heading: The Conjunction Operator
body: In logic, a conjunction asserts that two statements are both true. The most direct English translation is the word "and". If you have defined variable P and variable Q, the sentence "P and Q" translates to the symbolic expression $P \wedge Q$.

heading: The Contrast Rule
body: English uses words like "but", "yet", "however", and "although" to express contrast or surprise. Logic ignores this emotional narrative. If a sentence claims that two different facts are both true, it is a conjunction. Always translate these contrast words exactly like "and", using the $\wedge$ operator.

heading: Translating Contrast
body: Let S = It is sunny. Let C = It is cold. Notice how different English sentences map to the exact same logical expression.

examples.text: "It is sunny, and it is cold." -> S ∧ C
"It is sunny, but it is cold." -> S ∧ C
"It is sunny; however, it is cold." -> S ∧ C
"It is sunny, yet it is cold." -> S ∧ C

note: Despite different phrasing, all four sentences make the exact same logical claim.

heading: Implicit Conjunctions
body: Sometimes English joins two claims without any connecting word at all, relying entirely on punctuation. A semicolon joining two independent statements acts as an implicit conjunction. Because both statements are presented as true facts, they must be connected with the $\wedge$ operator.

heading: Punctuation as Conjunction
body: Let R = The alarm rang. Let W = I woke up.

examples.text: "The alarm rang; I woke up." -> R ∧ W
"The alarm rang, but I woke up." -> R ∧ W

note: Punctuation alone implies a conjunction if two standalone facts are asserted together.

heading: Key Takeaway
body: Translate "and", "but", "yet", "however", "although", and implicit punctuation joins using the conjunction operator $\wedge$. Logic only evaluates whether both connected statements are asserted as true.
